segments.s308-g97: طلایه (vanguard) is the subject; برافگند is a past, poetic/perfective verb meaning 'threw forth / spread out' with the prefix بَـ giving a simple past sense. بر گرد دشت is a prepositional phrase 'around the plain' where بر + گرد functions together as 'around' and دشت is the object 'plain'.
segments.s308-g98: همه شب is a temporal phrase 'all night' (همه = whole/entire + شب = night); همی is the durative/adverbial particle marking continuous action ('was continually / kept on'); گرد here is verbal (from گردیدن) meaning 'to go around / circle'; لشکر is the subject 'the army/host'; بگشت is intended as a past form of گشتن 'returned/turned/went about' but the provided sense list does not match this verb form, so that mapping is unavailable.
segments.s308-g99: به یک هفته ... درنگ is an adverbial temporal clause 'he stayed for a week / it was a week of tarrying': به + duration (یک هفته) marks the period; بودش is the past copula with enclitic -(ش) 'it/he was' (here impersonal/third-person), and آنجا درنگ is 'a stay there' (درنگ = tarrying/staying).
segments.s308-g100: همی ساخت is a durative past action: همی (durative particle) + ساخت (past of ساختن 'to make/construct') = 'kept making / made continually'; آرایش و ساز جنگ is a coordinated object meaning 'the array/arrangement and the gear/preparations of war' (آرایش = military array, ساز = equipment/means here, جنگ = war).
segments.s308-g101: به هشتم marks a point in time 'on the eighth (day)'; بیامد is the past tense 'came'; طلایه is the subject 'the vanguard'; ز راه is an ablative/instrumental phrase 'from/by the road' (ز = from, راه = road/way). Word order is typical: temporal phrase -> verb -> subject -> source phrase.
segments.s308-g102: به خسرو خبر داد is 'gave news to Khosrow' (به = to, خسرو = Khosrow, خبر = news/report, داد = gave); کآمد سپاه is a compact transmitted-content clause where کآمد (archaic/poetic) means 'that (it) came' or 'it came' and سپاه is the subject of that subordinate clause 'the army' — together: 'reported to Khosrow that the army had come.'
segments.s308-g103: سپه را is the definite object 'the army' marked by را; بدان سان is an archaic adverbial 'in that manner / so' (ب + دان + سان together form the phrase 'بدان‌سان' = 'in such a way'); بیاراست is a perfective/past or resultative verb 'arrayed/adorned' (به‌آراستن), and شاه is the agent 'the king' placed after the verb for poetic inversion.
segments.s308-g104: که introduces a result clause 'so that / so ... that'; نظاره گشتند is a nominal + verbal combination where نظاره (gaze/sight) is what becomes true and گشتند (past of گشتن) means 'they became / turned (to)' — here 'the sun and moon turned to gaze' (خورشید و ماه are the grammatical subjects). The construction is poetic: 'so arranged that sun and moon became observers.'
segments.s308-g25: This is a string of locative/temporal phrases that set the scene before the number: 'به جنگ' (to/in the fighting) + 'حصار اندرون' (inside the fortress). The numeral 'سی‌هزار' (thirty thousand) comes at the end as the quantity affected by that fighting. Word order is topical: location/scene first, then the quantity.
segments.s308-g26: 'همانا' is an emphatic particle ('verily/indeed') introducing the clause. 'که' links the emphasis to the clause that follows. 'شد کشته' is a passive verbal construction: 'شد' here functions as an auxiliary ('became/was') and 'کشته' is the past participle 'killed/slain' — together 'was killed/ended up slain'. 'در کارزار' is a locative complement 'in the battle/field'.
segments.s308-g27: 'همان بد' is an emphatic phrase 'that very bad/evil (one)'. 'که' introduces a relative clause. In the relative clause the predicate 'بیدادگر' (tyrant/cruel) comes before the copula 'بود' (was) and the head noun 'مرد' (man) follows the verb — a common poetic inversion; the whole means 'that very evil man who was a tyrant.'
segments.s308-g28: 'ورا' is the dative/benefactive pronoun 'to him'. The coordinated nouns 'دانش و بخت' ('learning/knowledge and fate/fortune') form the subjects of the following negated verb. 'یاری نکرد' is a two-word verb: 'یاری' (help) + 'نکرد' (did not do) = 'did not help'. The clause means 'knowledge and fate did not help him.'
segments.s308-g29: 'همه روی کشور' is a locative phrase meaning 'over the whole face/surface of the land' (i.e., everywhere in the country). 'سپه' is the subject 'the host/army' and 'گسترید' is a past/past-resultive form 'spread/has spread' — the clause states that the army is spread over the realm.
segments.s308-g30: 'شدست' is an archaic/poetic impersonal/predicative 'it became/it happened' used here with the following pronoun 'او' (he) to mean 'it came to pass that he...' or more simply 'he became'. 'کنون' is 'now', and 'از جهان ناپدید' is the phrase 'vanished from the world'. Together: 'he has now become vanished from the world.'
segments.s308-g31: 'از این پس' is a temporal phrase 'from now on / henceforth'. 'فرستم' is 1st-person singular present used here with future force: 'I will send'. 'به شاه آگهی' is the indirect object phrase 'news/information to the king' (literally 'to the king (a) news').
segments.s308-g32: 'ز روزی' (poetic 'از روزی') means 'from the day/when'. 'که' introduces the subordinate time-clause. 'باشد' is a present/subjunctive form meaning 'may be / will be' (used for an expected/future state). The sequence 'م را فرهی' should be read as 'مرا فرهی' = 'glory/splendor for me' — literally 'that there will be glory to me.' So the clause means 'when glory shall be mine.'
segments.s308-g17: This is a short narrative clause: وز آن پس (literally “from/after that”) sets the temporal frame, followed by the past simple verb برآمد “arose/issued forth.” یکی باد سخت is a noun phrase with indefinite یکی “a certain/one” modifying باد “wind” and سخت “severe/strong” as an adjective after the noun (typical classical word order). The whole segment means “after that there arose a strong wind.”
segments.s308-g18: که introduces a relative/subordinate clause “that/which.” The verb برکند is an infinitive/present-stem form meaning “uproot / pull out” used here with the sense of a single event (narrative perfective in older Persian poetry). شاداب is an adjective (“vigorous/fresh”) that directly modifies بیخ درخت “the root of the tree” (بیخ = root, درخت = tree); word order is verb + adjective + noun phrase, a common poetic arrangement.
segments.s308-g19: به آب اندر افتاد is a phrasal description of direction and action: به + آب + اندر (towards/into the water + within) followed by the past verb افتاد “fell.” چندی سپاه is the subject expression meaning “several / some hosts” (چندی = some/several, سپاه = army/host). The clause describes that a number of soldiers fell into the water.
segments.s308-g20: که again introduces a subordinate clause giving the cause or circumstance. جستند is the past plural of the verb meaning “they leaped/sprung” (here physical attack), followed by the preposition بر “upon/against” and the object ما “us.” یکی دستگاه is an appositive/quantifying phrase “one formation / a certain detachment” (یکی = one/a certain, دستگاه = formation/array). The clause as a whole: “which leapt upon us, a certain detachment.”
segments.s308-g21: به آوردگه در gives the locative frame (“to/at the place of arrival/venue”), with در reinforcing location. چنان شد is a fixed poetic construction meaning “it became so / it happened thus,” and سوار is the nominal predicate “horseman(s)/mounted men.” Together the clause states that at that venue there came to be many horsemen (lit. “so it became horsemen”).
segments.s308-g22: که introduces the explanatory/result clause. از ما یکی را is an object phrase “one of us” with را marking it as definite/object. شد is the past of شدن “became” and شکار here means “prey / quarry.” The numeric phrase دو صد (two hundred) is understood with شکار: the sense is “one of us became the prey of two hundred,” i.e. was overwhelmed by two hundred attackers.
segments.s308-g23: وز آن جایگه begins the clause with a locative/degenerative preface “from that place,” followed by the past verb رفت “went.” The next word ببهشت is an interjectional/poetic expression (here rendered as an exclamatory cry) and گنگ qualifies it as “muffled/indistinct.” Grammatically, the verb رفت governs the following nominal/exclamatory phrase describing what went/rose from the place.
segments.s308-g24: This is a noun phrase in apposition or continuation describing the scene: حصاری “a fortress/enclosure” (حصاری) followed by پر از مردم “full of people” (پر = full, از = of, مردم = people). The conjunction و then links جای تنگ “a narrow place/space” (جای = place, تنگ = narrow), giving two coordinating descriptions of the location.
segments.s308-g89: بدین سان is a fixed adverbial phrase meaning 'in this way/thus' formed from the prefix ب- + دین + سان; سپاهی بیامد is a simple past clause 'an army came'. ز گنگ is a prepositional phrase 'from Ganga' (here the river/region), placed after the verb as is common in classical Persian.
segments.s308-g90: که introduces a relative/nominal clause 'who/that...'; خورشید را uses the object marker را to mark 'the sun' as the object; آرزو کرد جنگ is a light-verb construction where آرزو (wish/desire) plus کرد (did/made) yields 'wished for war' (i.e., 'desired war').
segments.s308-g91: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'; بیرون شد is a two-word verbal phrase 'went out' (بیرون + شد); از شهر gives the origin 'from the city'. صف بر کشید is a verbal phrase 'drew up a line/formed ranks' where صف (battle-line) precedes the verb برکشید (poetic order).
segments.s308-g92: سوی introduces direction 'toward'; کوکها (here likely a place/landscape word in context) is the directional object, followed by لشکر اندر کشید, literally 'he drew the army inward' or 'he moved the host in that direction' — اندر + کشید places the verb's action into/among the army.
segments.s308-g93: میان دو لشکر is a prepositional phrase 'between the two armies'; دو منزل بماند uses دو + منزل ('two stages/encampments') as the subject and بماند (present/subjunctive/past-meaning 'remained' here) to state that two stopping-places lay between them. Word order places the locative phrase first for emphasis.
segments.s308-g94: جهاندار is the subject 'the sovereign/king'; گردنکشان را is the object with را marking 'the rebels/proud ones'; بخواند is a past-tense/formal narrative verb with the sense 'summoned/called', so the clause means 'the king summoned the rebels.'
segments.s308-g95: چنین introduces reported speech 'thus', and گفت is the reporting verb 'he said' (here presented as a narrator's verb introducing direct speech). کامشب is an adverb 'this night/tonight'; مجنبید is a negative plural imperative 'do not move' and هیچ strengthens the negation 'at all/anything'.
segments.s308-g96: نه خوب آید is a prophetic/forecasting clause 'no good will come' (نه + خوب + آید = 'not good comes'); آرامش اندر بسیچ places 'tranquility/peace' as the thing whose arrival is denied, اندر بسیچ meaning 'within the host/array'. The verb آید is present/future-ish 'will come' in narrative style.
segments.s308-g65: همه (subject) + بازگشتند (past verb, 3rd person plural) is a simple past clause meaning 'everyone returned/withdrew'. The prepositional phrase ز ایرانیان ('from the Iranians') follows the verb; ز here is the poetic variant of از marking origin or source.
segments.s308-g66: ببستند is past 3rd plural of بستن used idiomatically here to mean 'ceased/closed off' or 'brought to an end'. خون ریختن is a verbal-noun phrase 'the spilling/shedding of blood' acting as the object; را is the definite-object marker attaching to that noun phrase. میان at the end means 'between (them)' — an elliptical use where the full phrase (e.g. میانِ ایشان) is implied.
segments.s308-g67: چو at the start is a temporal conjunction 'when'. برداشَت is past 3rd singular of برداشتن here in the sense 'took up / mustered' (poetically 'raised'); افراسیاب is the subject. از ختن is a source phrase 'from Khotan' (ختن is a place name here, not the verb meaning 'circumcise').
segments.s308-g68: یکی لشکری is an indefinite nominal phrase 'a certain host/one army' (یکی = 'a certain / one'). شد is past 3rd singular of شدن used to form 'assembled / came to be'. بر + او means 'against him'; انجمن here names the result as a 'gathering/assembly' — idiomatically 'an army gathered against him.'
segments.s308-g69: که گفتی here introduces reported speech or citation: 'as you said'. زمین is the grammatical subject of the following predicate. برنتابد is an archaic/poetic negative-form verb meaning 'will not endure/bear' (3rd person); همی is an older adverb marking continuous or emphatic aspect ('still/indeed'), placed after the verb in classical style.
segments.s308-g70: ستاره (subject) + شمارش (the verbal noun 'counting' or 'reckoning') together form the idea 'the stars' reckoning/count of (him)'. نیابد is present/past-form of نیافتن/یافتن used here negative ('will not/cannot find/attain' → 'cannot reckon/count'); همی again adds a classical continuous/emphatic nuance. The clause thus means 'the stars cannot count him.'
segments.s308-g71: ز چین ('from China') is a source-phrase; چین here names the country China. سوی کیخسرو ('toward Kay Khosrow') gives the direction. آورد is past 3rd singular of آوردن 'brought' used idiomatically with روی ('face') to mean 'turned (his) face toward' — a common classical Persian idiom meaning 'he faced/turned to'.
segments.s308-g72: پر از درد is an adjectival phrase 'full of pain/sorrow' where پر = 'full (of)' and از introduces the cause/content. با لشکری modifies the verb with an accompaniment phrase 'with an army'; کینه‌جوی is an attributive adjective 'vengeful' qualifying لشکری. The whole phrase describes him as sorrowful and accompanied by a revenge-seeking force.
segments.s308-g57: که is a relative particle 'that/whom' introducing the clause. او را is the third-person pronoun with the definite-object marker را (object before the verb for emphasis). فرستاد is the simple past of فرستادن 'sent'. خاقان چین is the subject 'the Khaghan of China' placed after the verb by poetic inversion.
segments.s308-g58: به شاهی 'to kingship' marks the office conferred (شاهی = 'kingship'). بر او 'upon him' indicates the recipient. خواندند is past plural of خواندن here meaning 'they called/proclaimed', and آفرین is an interjection 'bravo' or acclamation which they uttered about him.
segments.s308-g59: همان 'that same' stresses identity. گنج 'treasure' is the noun, پیرانش is پیران 'elders' plus the possessive suffix -ش 'his' (pîrân + -š = 'his elders'). آمد 'came' and به دست is an idiom 'into the hand' = 'into his possession'.
segments.s308-g60: شتروار is an adverbial/quantifying adjective 'in camel-loads' (lit. camel-like loads). دینار is the coin 'dinar'. صدبار 'a hundred times' is an adverbial numeral. شست is past of شستن 'he washed' used idiomatically here for repeatedly handling/counting money; the subject is carried over from the previous clause.
segments.s308-g61: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when'. آن خواسته is 'that desired/asked-for thing'. برگرفت is the past of برگرفتن 'took back/removed' (poetic word order places the verb after the object). از marks 'from', and ختن is a place name (Khotan) not reflected in the provided sense list.
segments.s308-g62: سپاهی is an indefinite noun 'a troop/forces'. بیاورد is past tense 'he brought' (from آوردن). لشکر شکن is an epithet 'breaker of hosts/armies' placed after the verb as a descriptive apposition of the force brought.
segments.s308-g63: چو 'when' introduces the temporal clause. ز (poetic form of از) + این‌گونه = 'of this kind' or 'such'. آگاهی here means 'information/news' and آمد 'came' is the past verb, with به شاه showing the recipient 'to the king'.
segments.s308-g64: به نزدیک 'near' indicates the movement/position brought about. زنهار داده is a compound participial phrase ('having given warning' or 'having warned') with داده as the past participle of دادن. سپاه 'army' completes the clause; together the phrase means he warned and brought the army close.
segments.s308-g41: به introduces a circumstance ('in/on/for the hunt'); نخچیر here names the hunt/prey and functions as the head noun. یوزان (plural of یوز) and پرنده‑باز are coordinated (joined by و) and stand in apposition to نخچیر, specifying the agents of the hunt (cheetahs and falconers). There is no copula; the clause sets the scene adverbially.
segments.s308-g42: همه is a universal quantifier 'all/everyone'. مشک بویان is a compound-like phrase: مشک (musk) + بویان (agent/possessive form 'those with scent' / 'scent-bearers'), producing 'musk‑scented ones' or 'those smelling of musk'. بتان طراز is a descriptive phrase meaning 'idol‑fashioned' or 'idol‑like' (بتان = idols/beautiful ones; طراز used as a postpositive adjectival notion 'in the style of'), so the whole fragment describes everyone as musk‑scented and idol‑fashioned; the copula is implied.
segments.s308-g43: همه چارپایان is the subject 'all the quadrupeds'. به کردار introduces a simile/way of acting ('in the manner/behaviour of'); گور here is the animal 'onager' and modifies how the quadrupeds behaved. The phrase sets up a comparative behaviour (they acted like onagers).
segments.s308-g44: پراگند is an adjectival/predicative word 'scattered'. آگنده کردن is an infinitival/verbal construction with کردن ('to make/cause') — here the idea is a passive/resulting state '(were) made full/overrun' or more loosely 'were driven/filled' — and به زور ('by force') indicates the means. The clause is poetic and omits an explicit subject/copula; predicates attach back to the quadrupeds.
segments.s308-g45: به گردن uses به + noun to mark the locus or target ('at/on the neck'). به کردار introduces a comparative clause 'in the manner of'; شیران نر is a noun phrase 'male lions' (شیران plural, نر specifying male). The sequence describes how the animals were grasped/attacked — the verb is again omitted and understood from the context.
segments.s308-g46: به سان + noun is another simile 'like/as'. گوزنان (deer/stags) is the comparison class. به گوش و به سر lists two loci 'at the ear and at the head' using repeated به for emphasis/clarity. Word order is poetic; verbs are implied from the surrounding narrative.
segments.s308-g47: ز هر سو is a prepositional phrase 'from/at every side' (poetic ز = 'from'). فرستاد is the past tense 'he sent'. کارآگهان is a plural agent noun 'those aware/informed' (inspectors, scouts, or knowledgeable messengers) and is the object of فرستاد. The sequence means 'he dispatched informed men to all sides.'
segments.s308-g48: همی is a frequentative/adverbial particle 'continually/still'. جست here is used in the sense 'sought/searched' (not physical leaping). پیدا combines with جستن (پیدا جستن is a common compound) meaning 'to seek out / to discover', so همی جست پیدا = 'they constantly sought to find/discover'. ز کار جهان ('concerning the affairs of the world') specifies what they searched for: news or matters of the world.
segments.s308-g49: پس is an initial temporal connective meaning “then.” آگاهی آمد is a noun (آگاهی, “news/information”) followed by the past tense verb آمد (“came”); Persian often places the noun before the verb in such clauses. ز + place names (ز چین و ختن) is a poetic/preclassical form of از marking source/origin — “from China and Khotan.”
segments.s308-g50: The first از introduces the topic/agent of the news and here functions as “about/concerning” rather than strict physical origin; افراسیاب is a proper name. The second از repeats the same preposition to introduce another object (آن انجمن = “that assembly/meeting”), with آن as a demonstrative modifying انجمن.
segments.s308-g51: که here is the subordinating conjunction “that,” introducing the report. فغفور چین is a title + place-name construction (“the Faghfur of China”). با وی is a prepositional phrase “with him” indicating accompaniment. انباز is an archaic/uncertain lexical item (see senses) and forms a verbal compound with گشت (past of گشتن); گشت functions as the clause's finite verb indicating motion/return in this context.
segments.s308-g52: همه روی کشور is a subject-like phrase: همه (whole/entire) + روی (face/surface) + کشور (country) = “the whole face of the country.” پرآواز is a descriptive adjective meaning “renowned/celebrated” and it combines with the past verb گشت (“became”) to make the predicate “became renowned.” Note the typical noun–adjective/verb order in Persian narrative.
segments.s308-g53: ز چین تا به گلزریون marks an extent/range: “from China up to (as far as) Gulzarion,” with تا به used to indicate the endpoint. لشکرست is a nominal predicate: لشکر + the archaic contracted copula ست (classical form of است) — “(there) was a host/army.”
segments.s308-g54: بر ایشان is a prepositional phrase “over/on them.” چو is a comparative/simile particle “as/like,” introducing خاقان چین (“the Khagan of China”) as the comparison. سرورست is a noun (سرور, “lord/master”) with the same archaic copula -ست (= است), so the clause means “(he) was [their] lord/sovereign.”
segments.s308-g55: نداند is the negative present/past-form of دانستن and here functions as a general stative “knows not” (classical-style narration). The verb precedes کسی for emphasis/inversion, so نداند کسی = “no one knows.” راز آن خواسته is a genitive construction (“the secret of that request/wish”), with آن as the demonstrative and خواسته as the noun “what was asked/desired.”
segments.s308-g56: This is a coordinated noun phrase listing the items related to that secret: پرستنده (“the worshipper/devotee”) and اسب آراسته (“the adorned/decked horse”). و links the two nouns, and آراسته is an adjective (postpositive) modifying اسب.
segments.s308-g9: دگر گفت = 'another (person) said' (دگر here = 'another'); کز is the archaic subordinating conjunction 'that' introducing a reported clause; بخت functions adverbially 'by fate' (literally 'from/fate'), followed by the subject کاووس. The sequence places the circumstance ('بخت') before the proper name; the copula is omitted (typical in classical Persian).
segments.s308-g10: A string of predicative adjectives modifying کاووس: بزرگ 'great', جهاندیده 'worldly‑wise / experienced in the world', و linking to the final compound نیک‌پی. نیک‌پی is a compound meaning 'well‑born / of good lineage' (no copula expressed). The adjectives follow the (implicit) verb and agree in sense, forming a predicate group.
segments.s308-g11: گشاده شد is a verbal phrase 'became open / was opened' (شد as the past/inchoative auxiliary). The demonstrative آن plus the adjective گنگ ('mute' / 'dumb') precede the name افراسیاب; the verb is placed before the noun phrase (inversion common in poetry), so the clause reads 'that mute Afrasiab opened/was opened.'
segments.s308-g12: سر بخت او is a nominal phrase 'the head/chief of his fate' (سر = 'chief/leader' in the sense of principal part of fate). اندر آمد به خواب is a verbal phrase 'entered into [his] sleep' or idiomatically 'came to him in a dream' with اندر + آمد = 'came into' and به خواب specifying 'in a dream'; او is the indirect object 'to him.'
segments.s308-g13: به یک رزمگاه = 'to/into one battlefield' (به marking direction or setting). از نبرده سران literally 'from the not‑taken leaders' — نبرده is a past participial/negative compound meaning 'not taken/undefeated' (ne‑ + برده), so this phrase means 'from the leaders who had not been defeated.'
segments.s308-g14: سرافراز is an adjectival epithet 'honored / triumphant / lofty' qualifying the foregoing سران; با گرزهای گران is a prepositional phrase 'with heavy maces' (با = 'with', گرزهای = plural 'maces' with the plural suffix -های, گران = 'heavy/weighty'). The whole segment lists the proud, armed leaders entering the battlefield.
segments.s308-g15: همانا is an emphatic particle 'verily / truly.' که introduces the clause. افگنده شد is a passive/perfect construction 'were cast / were thrown' (افگنده = 'cast, thrown' and شد forming the passive/result). صد هزار is the object/quantity 'a hundred thousand' following the verb phrase (word order typical in poetic prose).
segments.s308-g16: به گلزریون marks the target/recipient of the action 'upon/against the Golzariun' (به used here as 'against' or 'upon'), and در یکی کارزار is an adverbial phrase 'in a single battle' (در = 'in', یکی = 'one/a certain', کارزار = 'battle'). The order places the target phrase before the locative phrase, both modifying افگنده شد.
segments.s308-g113: بر آنم که is an idiomatic clause meaning “I am of that mind / I intend that…”. بر is the preposition here with the demonstrative آن plus the 1st‑person suffix -م (آنم) to make the fixed expression بر آنم. که introduces the subordinate clause “that…”. از بخت is a prepositional phrase “from/of fortune”, and کیخسروست is the third‑person copula -ست attached to the name (poetic fused form) meaning “(belongs) to Kay Khosrow” or “is Kay Khosrow’s.”
segments.s308-g114: وگر is a conditional particle meaning “and if / but if.” بر سرم is a prepositional phrase literally “upon my head” with the enclitic -م marking first person; it functions idiomatically as “for me / upon me.” روزگاری is the subject “a time/age” and نوست is a poetic present/copula (نو + -ست) meaning “is new,” so the clause is a verbless/nominal clause with adjective‑copula order: Subject (روزگاری) + predicate (نوست).
segments.s308-g115: Again بر آنم که = “I am resolved that…”. با او شوم uses the verb شوم (1st‑person subjunctive/optative) after که to express the speaker’s intention: “that I may become / that I will be.” شوم + predicate noun همنبرد = “become a comrade‑in‑arms” (verb + nominal predicate). با marks accompaniment/association with او (“with him”).
segments.s308-g116: The two clauses are conditional. اگر کام یابم = “if I attain my desire”; یابم is the 1st‑person form of یافتن used here as subjunctive/optative in a conditional. The second اگر phrase, اگر مرگ و درد, is elliptical: the verb is omitted (common in poetry) and means “or if death and pain (befall me).” The second if therefore presents the alternative outcome without repeating a verb.
segments.s308-g117: بدو is the prepositional phrase 'to him' (be + pronoun), placed before the verb in inverted, poetic word order. گفت is a past‑tense verb ‘said’ (3rd person singular) taking the following noun phrase as its subject. هر کس که فرزانه بود is a relative clause: 'whoever was wise' (که introduces the relative clause; بود is past tense copula). The subject phrase follows the verb because of poetic inversion.
segments.s308-g118: گر … ار … forms a pair of conditional/contrastive particles functioning like “whether … or …” here. از خویش (خوی + possessive enclitic ش) means “of his own (people / kin)” with ش marking 'his'. بود repeats as the past copula in each alternative clause; ز بیگانه = “from/of strangers.” The structure gives two alternatives joined by ار.
segments.s308-g119: که‌گر (poetic fusion) introduces a generic conditional: 'whoever/if someone…'. شاه را uses the object marker را after the object 'king' (classic Persian marks definite direct objects). جست is a past/poetic form meaning 'sought / seeks' (here 'seeks'), and باید is the modal 'must'; نبرد here functions as the action 'fight/engage in battle' (a noun used as verbal action in older/poetic usage), so the clause reads 'whoever seeks the king must fight.'
segments.s308-g120: چرا باید is a rhetorical question 'why must…' with the verb of existence/necessity (باشد / be) left implicit. این لشکر و دار و برد is a coordinated list of objects (this army, this dar/realm/abode, and borst/booty‑gain) whose necessity the speaker questions; word order is demonstrative + nouns linked by و. The clause is elliptical — the verb is omitted but understood.
segments.s308-g73: چو at the start introduces a temporal subordinate clause ('when'). کیخسرو آگاه شد is a past-tense clause: آگاه (aware) + شد (became) = 'became aware' or 'learned'. زان سپاه uses زان (= 'of that / from that') to mark the thing he learned about — 'that army' (سپاه). The whole segment means 'when Kay Khosrow became aware of that army.'
segments.s308-g74: طلایه فرستاد is verb + object: طلایه (vanguard) + فرستاد (he sent/dispatched). چنـدی به راه is an adverbial phrase; چندی here means 'for a while / for some distance/time' and به راه ('on the road' / 'along the way') specifies the vanguard's movement. Together: 'he sent the vanguard forward along the road for a while.'
segments.s308-g75: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past verb meaning 'he commanded/ordered/said'. The following names are arranged elliptically: گودرز appears immediately after the verb (as the addressee or the first named person), and کشواد را uses the object marker را to mark Kashvad as a direct object of the command. The clause is deliberately terse/elliptical — the verb بفرمود governs the subsequent naming/appointment.
segments.s308-g76: سپهدار is a title ('commander') placed before the names that follow; گرگین و فرهاد را are the names with the object marker را. The construction relies on the previous verb (بفرمود) to supply 'appoint/make' so this segment reads as naming who is to hold the title: '(make) sepahdār — Gorgin and Farhad.'
segments.s308-g77: که introduces a subordinate clause (here a reported command/purpose: 'that ...'). ایدر (archaic form of 'here') + بباشید is a present-subjunctive/imperative plural 'be / remain (you pl.)'. با داد و رای is an adverbial phrase 'with justice and counsel' (داد = justice, رای = counsel/plan), modifying how they should 'be'.
segments.s308-g78: طلایه is the vanguard; شب و روز کرده is literally 'having made [it] night and day' — کرده is the past participle used here to indicate a continuous/established action connected to the command. به پای is an idiomatic phrase meaning 'on foot / on the march / along the road' (i.e., actively keeping watch or advancing). Together the phrase describes keeping the vanguard operating day and night on the road.
segments.s308-g79: به گودرز گفت = 'he said to Goudarz' (به marks the indirect object). این سپاه تو اند is a predicative clause: این سپاه (this army) + تو اند ('they are yours' / 'they belong to you'). اند is the old plural copula used with a collective noun like سپاه, and تو indicates possession ('yours').
segments.s308-g80: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. کار آید = 'when the affair/matter comes' (an idiom for 'when the need/arousal of action occurs'). اندر پناه تو اند = 'they are in your refuge/protection'; اندر is an archaic preposition 'in/within', پناه 'refuge/protection', and اند again the copula: 'they will be under your protection when need arises.'
segments.s308-g81: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of' and begins the prepositional phrase ز ترکان 'from the Turks'. هرآنگه که is a temporal/subordinating phrase 'whenever/whensoever that' introducing a subordinate clause; که here is the relative/connector 'who/that'. بینی is the 2nd-person present form 'you see' (classical/polite poetic form). یکی follows and identifies 'one (of them)' — the whole chunk reads 'from the Turks, whenever you see one'.
segments.s308-g82: که is the relative 'who/that'. یاد آرد is a verb phrase: یاد (a noun 'remembrance/trace') with آرد (archaic 3rd-singular 'brings/causes to come') together meaning 'brings to mind/recalls' (i.e. 'reminds'). از دشمنان اندکی is a prepositional phrase '(of) the enemies, a little' — اندکی qualifies the extent: 'even a little/at all'. Grammatically this completes the relative clause begun by که.
segments.s308-g83: هم is an adverb 'also/even' adding emphasis. اندر زمان is a temporal phrase 'in/at the time' (here 'when/at any time'). زنده is the adjective 'alive' modifying the implied person. بر دار کن is an imperative: بر + دار ('on the gallows') with کن (imperative 'do') — 'put [him] on the gallows' or 'hang [him]'. The clause orders an action conditional on the person being alive.
segments.s308-g84: دو پایش is a noun phrase 'his two feet' (دو 'two' + پای 'foot' + ش = his). ز بر سر is a positional phrase literally 'from/on (over) the head' or 'upon the head' specifying where the feet are placed. نگونسار is an adjective meaning 'mournful/woeful' functioning as a result/complement, and کن is the imperative 'do' — together the verb phrase instructs the agent to secure him by his two feet in a woeful (exposed) posture. Word order: object (دو پایش) + locative phrase (ز بر سر) + predicative adjective + imperative.
segments.s308-g85: چو introduces a conditional/temporal clause 'when/if'. بی‌رنج باشد is a subordinate clause 'is free from trouble' (بی‌رنج 'untroubled' + باشد classical 3rd‑person form 'be/is'). تو بی‌رنج باش is the main imperative addressed to 'you' (تو): 'you be untroubled'. The structure is a conditional followed by an instruction for the addressee.
segments.s308-g86: نگهبان is a predicate noun 'guardian/keeper' used as an instruction: '[be] guardian'. این لشکر و گنج is the direct object phrase 'this army and (its) treasure' (این 'this', لشکر 'army', و 'and', گنج 'treasure'). باش is the imperative 'be' — together: 'Be the guardian of this army and treasure.'
segments.s308-g87: تبیره is a noun meaning an 'interpretation/explanation/announcement' and is the subject. برآمد is the past tense 'arose/issued forth'. ز + پرده + سرای is a prepositional and genitive cluster 'from the curtain (of the house/room)' — together: 'An announcement arose from behind the curtain/curtain-room.' Note: classical word order places the prepositional origin after the verb.
segments.s308-g88: خروشیدن is a verbal noun/infinitive 'to roar/roaring' used here as the event that follows. زنگ 'ringing/bell' is one item listed, و is the coordinating 'and', هندی modifies an implied sound as 'Indian' (i.e. Indian-style cry/sound), and درای here functions as a predicate/complement indicating the presence/sounding (classical or idiomatic). Grammatically this lists the noises that rose: 'the ringing of bells and the Indian cry.'
segments.s308-g105: چو is the subordinating particle 'when'. افراسیاب is the subject (proper name). آن سپه را is a definite object phrase ('that host') marked by the object/topic marker را. بدید is the classical past form of دیدن ('he saw'), so the clause reads literally 'When Afrasiab saw that host...'.
segments.s308-g106: بیامد is the past tense 'he came' (he advanced). برابر here means 'opposite/against' and governs the following noun. صفی (here 'a/the array/line') is the battlefield line; the verb برکشید (literary past) 'raised/drew up' completes the action: 'he came and drew up a line opposite (them)'.
segments.s308-g107: به فرزانگان گفت = 'he said to the wise men' (به + indirect object + گفت). The following three nouns (کین دشت رزم) form the topic of what he said: 'this plain/field of battle' (poetic word order places modifiers together).
segments.s308-g108: به دل مر مرا is a compressed, emphatic locative/dative phrasing: به دل = 'in the heart', مر / مرا are emphatic dative/accusative pronouns ('to me / me') used pleonastically for emphasis. م (written here for است) is the archaic/poetic copula 'is'. چون introduces a simile 'as/like', خرامست is the predicate ('is like proud striding / a stately parade'), and و بزم adds a coordinated noun 'and a feast/assembly'.
segments.s308-g109: مرا شاد بر گاه خواب آمدی: مرا ('to me' / 'me') is the patient/recipient, شاد is the predicate adjective 'happy/joyful', بر گاه خواب = 'at the time/hour of sleep', and آمدی is the past 2nd-person form 'you came' — a direct address (fortune or some addressee is being spoken to).
segments.s308-g110: چو = 'when'. رزمم is 'my battle' (رزم + enclitic -م). نبودی is the archaic past negative 'was not' (negated past of بودن). شتاب modifies آمدی: 'you came with haste' or 'haste came' — here it indicates that the arrival was hasty.
segments.s308-g111: کنون 'now' sets the time. مانده is a past participle/remaining ('left, remained'), گشتم is the past 'I became/was turned', and چنین در گریز = 'thus, into flight/flight' (در گریز = 'in flight/flighty state'). Together they mean 'now I am left thus, turned to flight.'
segments.s308-g112: سری پر ز کینه = 'a head full of hate/grudge' (سری = head; پر ز = 'full of'; کینه = 'hatred/grudge'). دلی پرستیز = 'a heart full of pride/pomp' (دلی = heart-related adjective form; پرستیز = 'prestige/pomp' in the poetic sense). This is a compact nominative phrase describing his mental state.
segments.s308-g1: دبیر نویسنده را پیش خواند is a simple past clause: دبیر (subject) + نویسنده را (direct object marked by را) + پیش خواند (verb). پیش here has the sense 'forward/in the presence of' and خواند is the past tense 'called/summoned'. Word order is S‑O‑V as normal in classical Persian; را marks 'نویسنده' as the definite object.
segments.s308-g2: سخن هرچه بایست با او براند is an instruction clause. سخن هرچه = 'whatever speech/words', بایست is the modal 'ought/must' modifying the clause that follows, با او = 'to/with him', and براند (third‑person present/optative of راندن in classical usage) expresses the action 'let him carry/put/utter'. The sequence places the object phrase 'سخن هرچه' before the modal and verb, typical of older Persian.
segments.s308-g3: سر نامه کرد آفرین از نخست is a compact phrase meaning 'he made (کرد) praise (آفرین) the head/preface (سر نامه) from the first (از نخست)'. سر نامه is a compound 'head of the book' = preface; کرد is simple past 'made/wrote'; آفرین here functions as a noun 'praise/acclamation', and از نخست means 'from the outset'.
segments.s308-g4: بدان کو زمین از بدیها بشست is a classical relative phrase: بدان کو = به + آن + کو → 'to/for the one who' (ب as 'to/for', دان here part of the pronominal/archaic form, and کو functioning as a relative 'who/which'), زمین از بدیها = 'the earth from evils' (object of the verb), and بشست is simple past 'he washed/cleansed'. Together: 'for the one who washed the earth of evils.'
segments.s308-g5: چنان اختر خفته بیدار کرد: چنان 'thus/so' modifies the whole action; اختر خفته is a noun plus past participle 'the sleeping star'; بیدار کرد is a causative/perfective expression 'made (it) awake' or 'awakened (it)' with کرد functioning as the past tense auxiliary/verb meaning 'caused/made'. The order (adverb – subject phrase – verb) is typical.
segments.s308-g6: سر جادوان را نگونسار کرد has سر جادوان را as the definite object ('the heads of the magicians' marked by را), نگونسار an adjective meaning 'mournful/woeful', and کرد 'made' (past). The construction 'X را Y کرد' means 'made X Y' (caused X to be Y). جادوان is plural 'magicians/enchanters'.
segments.s308-g7: توانایی و دانش و داد از اوست is a nominal sentence listing three nouns coordinated by و (توانایی, دانش, داد) followed by از اوست ('are from him' / 'belong to him'). ست is the archaic copula (classical form of است). The coordination places qualities in parallel and the prepositional phrase از او identifies their source/owner.
segments.s308-g8: به گیتی ستم یافته شاد از اوست is another nominal clause: به گیتی ('in/regarding the world') + ستم یافته (passive participle 'oppressed' = 'having suffered oppression') + شاد از اوست ('is glad because of him'). The structure uses the participle یافته to form the passive adjective and شاد ... است to state the resulting emotional state, with از او indicating cause/agent.
segments.s308-g33: از آن پس is a temporal phrase meaning 'after that' (from + demonstrative + 'after'); بیامد is a classical/poetic past tense 'came' (3rd person). به شادی marks manner ('in/with joy') and نشست is the past tense verb 'sat down' — together 'came in joy and sat down'.
segments.s308-g34: پری روی is a nominal compound 'fair‑faced (one)' (پری 'fairy/beautiful one' + روی 'face'). پیش اندرون means 'before/in front of the inner apartments' (پیش = in the presence of/before; اندرون = inner quarters). می‌به دست is an adverbial/ablative phrase 'with mey‑beh in (her) hand' — می‌به is a poetic drink (wine-and‑quince) and دست 'hand' follows it.
segments.s308-g35: ببد is an archaic/poetic past form of 'to be' meaning 'it was' or 'it became'. تا here links the clause in a comparative/result sense ('so that / as if'), بهار is 'spring', اندرآورد is the past tense 'brought in/brought forth', and روی is the object 'face/appearance' — 'it was as if spring brought forth a face.'
segments.s308-g36: جهان شد = 'the world became' (subject + past of شدن). بهشتی is a predicative adjective 'heavenly / like paradise'. پر از is the standard construction 'full of' (پر + از) and is followed by the coordinated nouns رنگ و بوی 'color and scent'.
segments.s308-g37: همه دشت is the subject 'the entire plain'. چون introduces a simile 'like/as', and پرنیان is an adjective 'silken' (literally 'like silken cloth'). شد is the past 'became'. به رنگ here indicates the resulting appearance 'in color' (به marking the state/result).
segments.s308-g38: هوا گشت = 'the air turned/changed' (گشت as 'became/turned'). بر سان is a comparative construction 'upon/like' (بر = on/upon; سان = like/as), and پشت پلنگ is the image 'the back of a leopard' — together: 'the air turned as on a leopard's back.'
segments.s308-g39: گرازیدن here is a verbal noun/verb meaning 'to charge/run' (often with a sense of trampling or rushing). گور in this context is the animal 'onager/wild ass' (not 'grave'), joined with و 'and' and آهو 'gazelle' as the animated subjects/objects; به دشت marks direction 'into/across the plain'.
segments.s308-g40: بدین گونه is the fixed phrase 'in this manner/thus' (here presented in two pieces in the gloss; بدین = 'by this/thus', گونه = 'manner/way'). بر چند خوشی گذشت is a clause with گذشت 'passed' (past); بر چند خوشی = 'over/on a few pleasures' i.e. 'several pleasant moments/days passed.'
segments.s308-g121: The phrase puts the plural nominal subject 'همه چین و توران' ('all China and Turan') before the locative phrase 'به پیش' ('to/in the presence of; brought forward') and finishes with the modal verb 'تواند' (3rd person singular 'can/is able'). Word order is Subject + locative/adverbial + modal verb; 'چین' and 'توران' are proper place-names.
segments.s308-g122: This is a short conditional/alternative clause built with the poetic preposition 'ز' ('from') and the archaic conjunction 'ار' ('if' / 'whether'). The structure 'ز بیگانگان ار ز خویش ش تواند' presents two sources ('from foreigners' vs. 'from his own') joined by 'ار' to mean 'whether... or...', with the same verb 'تواند' understood as governing both alternatives.
segments.s308-g123: 'فدای تو بادا' is an optative formula: 'فدایِ تو' = 'a sacrifice for you' and 'بادا' expresses 'may it be'. The noun phrase 'همه جان ما' ('all our lives') is the thing being offered; the order is emphatic/poetic (object/phrase + optative + clarifying noun).
segments.s308-g124: 'چنین بود' is a simple past copular clause 'it was thus/so.' The following 'تا بود پیمان ما' is a temporal clause with 'تا' = 'until/so long as' and a second past copula 'بود' ('was'), meaning 'so long as our covenant existed.' The two 'بود' forms are independent: first as statement, second inside the temporal clause.
segments.s308-g125: The clause begins with the conditional 'اگر' ('if/even if') followed by the verb 'شود' ('becomes/happens') to set a hypothetical. Here the following noun phrase (implied quantity) is the hypothetical victim count; the compressed poetic expression lets 'کشته‌گر' stand in the cluster referring to killing/slaughter in the hypothetical situation.
segments.s308-g126: This is an admonition whose core is '...خوار مایه مدار' — an idiomatic prohibition 'do not make (them) a cause of contempt.' 'تن خویش را' ('your person/people' with the object marker 'را') is the direct object being protected: 'do not treat your own people as worthless.' Note: 'مدار' here functions poetically as the prohibitive/imperative element; the noun 'مایه' ('cause') follows the adjective 'خوار' ('despised/worthless') to yield 'a cause/source of contempt.'
segments.s308-g127: 'همه سر به سر' is a reduplicated/idiomatic intensifier meaning 'all, completely/every one' (literally 'head to head' but used adverbially). It is followed by the predicate noun 'نیکخواه' ('well-wisher') and the suffixed verb-form 'توایم' ('I am yours' or 'I am with you') in which the first-person singular is fused with the predication (archaic/poetic).
segments.s308-g128: The relative/causal 'که' (= 'for/that/as') introduces the explanatory clause 'زنده به فر کلاه توایم.' 'زنده' = 'alive'; the phrase 'به فر کلاه' links two honorific nouns ('فر' royal glory, 'کلاه' literally 'hat' but here figuratively 'crown'/honour) with the preposition 'به' ('by/with'), and 'توایم' again expresses the speaker's belonging/loyalty: 'as long as we live, by the glory of your crown we are yours.'

sentences.s308-s49: The vanguard deployed around the plain; all night long the host kept circling about.
sentences.s308-s50: He remained there for a week; during that time he made the array and prepared the instruments of war.
sentences.s308-s51: On the eighth (day) the vanguard came by the road and reported to Khosrow that the army had arrived.
sentences.s308-s52: The king arrayed the host in such a manner that the sun and moon turned to behold it.
sentences.s308-s13: Thirty thousand were slain inside the fortress in the fighting.
sentences.s308-s14: He was that very wicked man who was a tyrant, and neither learning nor fortune helped him.
sentences.s308-s15: The host spread across the whole country; he has now disappeared from the world.
sentences.s308-s16: From this time on I will send the king news of the day when glory will be mine.
sentences.s308-s9: After that there rose a strong wind that uprooted the vigorous root of a tree.
sentences.s308-s10: Some troops fell into the water, since a troop sprang upon us.
sentences.s308-s11: At the entrance-place so many horsemen appeared that one of us became the prey of two hundred.
sentences.s308-s12: From that place went forth a muffled exclamation: a fortress full of people and a narrow space.
sentences.s308-s45: Thus an army came from the Ganges, who desired war with the Sun.
sentences.s308-s46: When he went out of the city he drew up a formation; he moved the army toward the heights.
sentences.s308-s47: Two encampments lay between the two armies; the sovereign summoned the rebels.
sentences.s308-s48: Thus he said: 'Tonight do not move at all; no good will come of calm among the host.'
sentences.s308-s33: They all withdrew from the Iranians; they stopped the shedding of blood between them.
sentences.s308-s34: When Afrasiab mustered from Khotan, a host of troops gathered against him.
sentences.s308-s35: As you said, the earth will not bear him, nor will the stars be able to count him.
sentences.s308-s36: From China he turned his face toward Kay Khosrow, his face full of sorrow, with a vengeful army.
sentences.s308-s29: That whom the Khaghan of China sent, they proclaimed him king and cried "Afarin" ("Bravo").
sentences.s308-s30: That very treasure of his elders came into his hands; he washed (handled) dinars in camel-loads a hundred times.
sentences.s308-s31: When that desired thing was taken back from Khotan, he brought troops — an army-breaker.
sentences.s308-s32: When such news reached the king, he gave a warning and drew his army near.
sentences.s308-s21: In the hunt — by cheetahs and falconers — all were musk‑scented and fashioned like idols.
sentences.s308-s22: All the four‑footed beasts, behaving like onagers, scattered and were driven off by force.
sentences.s308-s23: They seized (them) by the neck like male lions, and (seized) like stags at the ear and at the head.
sentences.s308-s24: He sent informed men in every direction; they continually searched out news of the affairs of the world.
sentences.s308-s25: Then news came from China and Khotan about Afrasiab and that assembly.
sentences.s308-s26: That the Faghfur (emperor) of China returned with him (انباز گشت), and the whole face of the country became renowned.
sentences.s308-s27: From China as far as Gulzarion there stood a host; over them, like the Khagan of China, was their sovereign.
sentences.s308-s28: No one knows the secret of that request — the worshipper and the decorated horse.
sentences.s308-s5: Another said that, by fate, Kavus was great, worldly-wise, and well-born.
sentences.s308-s6: That mute Afrasiab opened (became open), and the head of his fate came to him in a dream.
sentences.s308-s7: To a single battlefield, from the unconquered leaders, the proud (triumphant) ones, with heavy maces.
sentences.s308-s8: Verily, a hundred thousand were cast upon the Golzarion in a single battle.
sentences.s308-s57: I am resolved that my fortune is tied to Kay Khosrow, and if a new time comes upon me.
sentences.s308-s58: I intend that I become a fellow warrior with him — if I attain my desire, or if death and suffering (befall).
sentences.s308-s59: To him said every one who was wise: whether he was of his own people or of strangers.
sentences.s308-s60: For whoever seeks the king must fight; why should this army, this realm and these spoils be (needed)?
sentences.s308-s37: When Kay Khosrow learned of that army, he sent a vanguard forward along the road for a while.
sentences.s308-s38: He commanded (as follows): Goudarz, Kashvad; the sepahdār — Gorgin and Farhad (were assigned).
sentences.s308-s39: — that you remain here with justice and counsel, keeping the vanguard on the road night and day.
sentences.s308-s40: He said to Goudarz: 'This army is yours; when the need arises they are under your protection.'
sentences.s308-s41: From among the Turks, whenever you see even one who in any way recalls the enemies a little.
sentences.s308-s42: Even while he is alive, hang him on the gallows; fix his two feet up over his head and make him woeful.
sentences.s308-s43: If he is untroubled, you be untroubled; be the guardian of this army and of the treasure.
sentences.s308-s44: An announcement rose from behind the curtain; the ringing of bells and the Indian cry resounded.
sentences.s308-s53: When Afrasiab saw that host, he came forward and drew up a line opposite them.
sentences.s308-s54: He said to the wise men: 'This plain of battle — in my heart it is to me like a proud procession and a feast.'
sentences.s308-s55: Joy came to me at the hour of sleep; when my battle was not (ready), you came in haste.
sentences.s308-s56: Now I remain thus, turned to flight — a head full of rancor, a heart full of pride.
sentences.s308-s1: The secretary summoned the scribe forward and told him to speak whatever needed to be said to him.
sentences.s308-s2: He prefixed the book with praise from the very beginning, for the one who washed the earth clean of evils.
sentences.s308-s3: Thus he roused the sleeping star and made the heads of the magicians woeful.
sentences.s308-s4: Ability, knowledge, and justice are his; the oppressed world is joyful because of him.
sentences.s308-s17: After that she came in joy and sat down; the fair‑faced woman took a seat before the inner apartments with a cup of mey‑beh in her hand.
sentences.s308-s18: It was as if spring had brought forth its face; the world became a paradise full of color and scent.
sentences.s308-s19: The whole plain became like silken cloth in color; the air turned as if upon a leopard's back.
sentences.s308-s20: The onagers and gazelles charged across the plain; in this fashion several pleasant moments passed.
sentences.s308-s61: All China and Turan can come forward — whether (they come) from foreigners or from his own [people].
sentences.s308-s62: May all our lives be sacrificed for you; it was so while our covenant lasted.
sentences.s308-s63: Even if a hundred are slain, do not regard a hundred thousand of your own as worthless.
sentences.s308-s64: We are all, every one without exception, your well-wishers; while we live, by the glory of your crown we are yours.
